title: Carded and Discarded

jokes.text: Do you actually say the words 'hot dog'? / Yes, I do.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso claims he enthusiastically says 'Hot dog, let me at 'em!' every morning
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's incredulous question reveals how ridiculous this sounds, and his earnest confirmation makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat - reveals Mr. Rosso's earnest awkwardness through specific, quotable dialogue. Lindsay's deadpan response is the perfect straight-man setup.

jokes.text: A lot of times you might see, maybe you're taking a shower or something, and you go, 'What are these?' 'What's that?'
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso is trying to relate to teenagers about puberty struggles
jokes.punchline: His awkward, vague reference to discovering body parts in the shower
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy - adult trying too hard to be relatable to teens with inappropriate TMI moment.

jokes.text: I'M A BOY OR GIRL, AND I'M A MAN OR WOMAN.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso is performing Alice Cooper's '18' to connect with the kids
jokes.punchline: He awkwardly tries to make the lyrics gender-inclusive mid-song
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Mr. Rosso's overly-PC tendencies ruining a rock song. The awkward self-correction mid-performance is perfectly cringe.

jokes.text: That was brutal. I've never hated Alice Cooper as much as I do right now.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso just finished his awkward, modified version of Alice Cooper's '18'
jokes.punchline: Daniel's blunt assessment of how bad the performance was
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan reaction. Daniel's matter-of-fact delivery of this harsh but accurate assessment gets the laugh.

jokes.text: You know who didn't go to college? Einstein. Thomas Edison. / [INDULGENT SIGH] / Frank. / Frank who? / The dude who pumps my gas.
jokes.setup: Lindsay lists famous people who didn't go to college to justify her choices
jokes.punchline: After Einstein and Edison, she adds 'Frank' - just some random gas station attendant
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke with perfect structure. The buildup with prestigious names makes the mundane punchline land harder. Great character moment showing Lindsay's rebellious logic.

jokes.text: Through the mail? She's lucky that wasn't intercepted by some junkie working for the Post Office.
jokes.setup: Lindsay receives $300 in the mail for her college fund
jokes.punchline: Harold's paranoid assumption about postal workers being drug addicts
jokes.explanation: Shows Harold's paranoid worldview. The specificity of 'junkie working for the Post Office' makes it character-specific rather than generic paranoia.

jokes.text: So we all sit around the table, and you yell at us? / You do that to us every night.
jokes.setup: Harold describes the family game Pit as everyone yelling at each other
jokes.punchline: Lindsay points out he already does this every night anyway
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about family dynamics. Lindsay's comeback perfectly captures the irony of Harold trying to sell a 'fun' activity that sounds like their regular dinner table.

jokes.text: When you two were kids, you used to run around naked and lay in bed with us all night. We bathed you, and we cleaned your butts when you pooped. And we loved it.
jokes.setup: Harold is complaining about his kids not wanting to spend time with the family
jokes.punchline: His increasingly uncomfortable list of intimate childhood care, ending with 'And we loved it'
jokes.explanation: Classic parental guilt trip taken too far. The escalating intimacy and his enthusiastic 'And we loved it!' makes it appropriately uncomfortable.

jokes.text: We are going to be close from now on, whether you like it or not. We are going to spend quality time together, and we're going to enjoy it, damn it! The Weirs are playing Pit this Friday. Get used to it.
jokes.setup: Harold's emotional speech about family togetherness
jokes.punchline: The contradiction of forcing enjoyment and ending with a militaristic command about a board game
jokes.explanation: Perfect Harold moment - the irony of demanding forced fun, culminating in the absurd declaration about Pit. The anger over a board game is both pathetic and relatable.

jokes.text: Well, that might be Mr. Darwin now.
jokes.setup: Teacher is explaining natural selection when there's a knock on the door
jokes.punchline: Pretends it might be Darwin himself at the door
jokes.explanation: Simple teacher joke - the kind of dad humor that gets eye rolls but mild chuckles. Economy saves it from being completely forgettable.

jokes.text: Always room for another head on the chopping block.
jokes.setup: New student enters the biology class
jokes.punchline: Teacher welcomes her with a morbid metaphor about execution
jokes.explanation: Dark teacher humor that establishes character. The casual morbidity contrasts nicely with the mundane classroom setting.

jokes.text: I saw Ronald Reagan kissing a monkey on TV last night. / On the news? / No, no, no. It was in a movie. He's a pretty good actor. So was the monkey...
jokes.setup: Bill mentions seeing Reagan kissing a monkey, Sam assumes it's news
jokes.punchline: The clarification that it was a movie, followed by the earnest review of both Reagan and the monkey's acting
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection with Bill's innocent movie review delivery. The equal treatment of Reagan and monkey as actors is perfectly Bill-like naivety.

jokes.text: Really? My dad drives a Coup de Ville, so we're practically related.
jokes.setup: Maureen mentions her dad works for General Motors
jokes.punchline: Neal's illogical conclusion that driving a GM car makes them practically related
jokes.explanation: Perfect Neal logic - making tenuous connections to seem interesting. The earnest delivery of obviously flawed reasoning is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Once he ran over an old lady, and then he blamed it on his car. He said the accelerator got stuck.
jokes.setup: Discussion about weird teachers
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of their teacher running over an elderly person and making excuses
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered matter-of-factly. The casual way Sam presents this horrific information gets the uncomfortable laugh.

jokes.text: Our shop teacher got his hair caught in a lathe and then he got his whole scalp torn off and now he has to wear a wig. / Our shop teacher got his pinky cut off by the radial arm saw. And when they tried to sew it back on, it didn't fit anymore. Now everyone calls him Pinky Tuskadero.
jokes.setup: Trading stories about teachers with injuries
jokes.punchline: Escalating from scalping to severed finger, ending with the nickname 'Pinky Tuskadero'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of gruesome teacher stories. 'Pinky Tuskadero' is a brilliant nickname that makes the dark humor more palatable and memorable.

jokes.text: But if I start bugging you, please, be sure and let me know. / Oh, don't worry. We'd never let you bug us.
jokes.setup: Maureen politely asks the geeks to tell her if she's bothering them
jokes.punchline: Sam's response implies they would definitely tell her, contradicting social norms
jokes.explanation: Shows the geeks' social awkwardness - not understanding that you're supposed to politely lie in this situation.

jokes.text: You'd better shut up. / [LAUGHS] Just 'cause you got a fake I.D., don't go lording it over the rest of us.
jokes.setup: Someone mentions going to see a band at a bar
jokes.punchline: The casual threat followed by Ken's laugh and accusation about fake ID privilege
jokes.explanation: Shows the dynamic between the freaks - Daniel's mock aggression and Ken's knowing response about fake ID status among teens.

jokes.text: This looks exactly like you. / I know. / Except it says you're 24. / So? I look mature. Nobody ever stops me.
jokes.setup: Examining Kim's fake ID that looks like her
jokes.punchline: Kim's confident assertion that she looks mature enough to pass for 24
jokes.explanation: Kim's delusional confidence about looking older. Standard teen fake ID humor but fits her character's bravado.

jokes.text: [ALL LAUGH] Some stupid bouncer asked what my star sign was. I didn't know, so he snagged it. / Aw. It's Capricorn! Cancer! Capricorn! Oh!
jokes.setup: Daniel explains how he lost his fake ID
jokes.punchline: Ken immediately knows his astrological sign, which Daniel obviously doesn't
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff showing Daniel's lack of basic knowledge that got him caught, contrasted with Ken's immediate knowledge of what Daniel should have known.

jokes.text: Dude, I hate astrology. What, everybody born in the same month is gonna have the same life?
jokes.setup: Discussion about astrology and star signs
jokes.punchline: Daniel's logical but obvious observation about astrology's flaws
jokes.explanation: Daniel's rare moment of logical thinking, though it's a pretty obvious observation. Shows character development in a small way.

jokes.text: Well, well, well. I don't know why, but I'm liking her more and more every day. / Join the club. / Ew.
jokes.setup: Lindsay offers to lend money for fake IDs
jokes.punchline: Daniel's growing appreciation, Nick's agreement, Kim's disgusted reaction
jokes.explanation: Shows the group dynamics with Lindsay - Daniel and Nick warming to her usefulness, Kim still territorial. Kim's 'ew' reaction gets the laugh.

jokes.text: You should stop by the A.V. room. We just got a new beta machine. / Thank you, Harris. We're very late, though.
jokes.setup: Harris tries to impress Maureen with A.V. equipment
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's polite but dismissive brush-off
jokes.explanation: Classic geek attempt to impress with technology, met with polite rejection. Shows social hierarchy dynamics.

jokes.text: Tomorrow's pizza day in the cafeteria. / Yeah. It's made out of real tomato sauce and everything.
jokes.setup: Gordon announces pizza day to Maureen
jokes.punchline: Harris's enthusiastic emphasis on 'real tomato sauce' as if this is remarkable
jokes.explanation: Perfect geek enthusiasm for mundane things. Harris treating 'real tomato sauce' as a selling point reveals their low standards for excitement.

jokes.text: I don't have to be home ever.
jokes.setup: Kids discussing when they have to be home
jokes.punchline: Sam's matter-of-fact statement implying parental neglect
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered casually. The economy of the line and Sam's deadpan delivery makes this unexpectedly funny and sad.

jokes.text: We need some fake I.D.s. / Shh. I work here. / Come here. / I mean, we need some slacks.
jokes.setup: Daniel loudly asks for fake IDs in a department store
jokes.punchline: Howie's panic and Daniel's obvious cover story about needing 'slacks'
jokes.explanation: Shows Daniel's casual approach to illegal activities contrasted with Howie's paranoia. 'Slacks' as a cover is appropriately absurd.

jokes.text: Astrology. It's not astrology. It's you, man. It's facts, it's who you're supposed to be. June--Cancer, Libra--October, Capricorn--December. Know it. 1958. You are them.
jokes.setup: Daniel dismisses astrology
jokes.punchline: Howie's intense, nonsensical lecture about astrological identity
jokes.explanation: Howie's passionate but confused astrology defense. The intensity and scrambled logic make it funny, showing his character as someone who takes weird things seriously.

jokes.text: A chick with money. You got good taste, Stretch.
jokes.setup: Lindsay offers to pay for the fake IDs
jokes.punchline: Howie's creepy compliment to Daniel about Lindsay
jokes.explanation: Creepy Howie being inappropriate about Lindsay. The 'Stretch' nickname adds to the uncomfortable vibe he creates.

jokes.text: Had to bring the big rocket, didn't you? / What? So I got a big rocket. What am I supposed to do, cut it in half?
jokes.setup: Neal complains about Bill's large model rocket
jokes.punchline: Bill's defensive response about his rocket size
jokes.explanation: Mild sexual innuendo with model rockets. Bill's innocent defensiveness works with his character, though the joke is pretty obvious.

jokes.text: She is beautiful. I can't believe she's gonna hang out with us. / What if I'm asleep right now and this is just a dream, and any moment Maureen and I are gonna make love? / Ew. / Then what are we doing here? / Maybe this is my nightmare.
jokes.setup: Neal's innocent appreciation of Maureen
jokes.punchline: Bill's sexual fantasy, leading to Sam's perfect comeback about being in Bill's nightmare
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from sweet to creepy to Sam's perfect logical comeback. Shows each character's personality and relationship dynamics perfectly.

jokes.text: God, Bill, your rocket's huge. / Oh, it is? I hadn't noticed.
jokes.setup: Maureen compliments Bill's model rocket
jokes.punchline: Bill's obviously false modesty about not noticing his rocket's size
jokes.explanation: Bill's transparent false modesty. The double entendre potential and his obvious pride in his rocket size makes it character-appropriate humor.

jokes.text: Yeah, but you're definitely more than friends. / I don't know. We only kissed once, and it's not-- It's not a big deal.
jokes.setup: Kim probes Lindsay about her relationship with Nick
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's awkward downplaying of their kiss
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's awkward navigation of relationship questions. The uncomfortable deflection shows her inexperience with these situations.

jokes.text: We're not into labelling things, you know? We have a more mature relationship than that. / Let's just say we have an understanding. / Well, I'm glad you have an understanding, 'cause I don't know what the hell you're talking about.
jokes.setup: Nick describes his relationship with Lindsay in vague, pretentious terms
jokes.punchline: Daniel's blunt response that Nick's explanation makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Nick's pretentious romanticism vs. Daniel's pragmatic honesty. Daniel's cutting response punctures Nick's nonsense beautifully.

jokes.text: She's the kind of woman you could cut the cheese in front of. / Oh, yeah, Bill, that's what love is all about. / It is. I mean, you couldn't be in love with someone if you couldn't.
jokes.setup: Bill defines the ideal girlfriend as someone you can fart in front of
jokes.punchline: Bill's earnest defense of farting as a measure of true love
jokes.explanation: Classic Bill - reducing romance to bodily functions. His earnest belief in this as a relationship truth is both cringe and endearing.

jokes.text: What do you think you do, get up and run outside every time you have to let one rip? You'd never get any sleep.
jokes.setup: Sam argues you can't fart in front of women
jokes.punchline: Neal's logical progression of the impossibility of hiding all flatulence in marriage
jokes.explanation: Neal's practical analysis of the farting situation. The visual of constantly running outside and sleep deprivation makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You see the way she looks at me? She can barely contain herself. / You just happen to be looking at her every time she's thinking about me.
jokes.setup: Bill claims Maureen looks at him with obvious attraction
jokes.punchline: Neal's alternative explanation that Bill only notices when she's thinking about Neal
jokes.explanation: Good rival banter showing how each geek interprets the same evidence differently. Neal's counter-interpretation is clever and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: The same way we decided who had to stand in line for The Empire Strikes Back tickets. / I'll get the hat.
jokes.setup: They need to decide who gets to ask out Maureen
jokes.punchline: Reference to their established method of drawing lots, with Bill immediately fetching the hat
jokes.explanation: Shows their established friendship dynamics and how they solve disputes fairly. The Star Wars reference grounds it in the era.

jokes.text: Did you know that in some cultures, if your children shame you, you're allowed to have them executed? / Well, I feel shamed. / Oh, Harold.
jokes.setup: Jean suggests Harold can't force the kids to spend time with them
jokes.punchline: Harold's casual mention of child execution as a parenting option
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark Harold humor - taking parental frustration to its logical extreme. Jean's weary response shows this is typical Harold behavior.

jokes.text: Honey, it's like that old saying-- If you love something, set it free. Maybe if we try not to force it, they'll come to us. / Well, all right. There you go. They're free. Let 'em free. Let 'em go. [LAUGHS] See? / Get back here.
jokes.setup: Jean offers wise advice about letting children come to them naturally
jokes.punchline: Harold immediately contradicts this by calling the kids back
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Harold's inability to follow good advice even for five seconds. The immediate contradiction is perfectly timed and reveals his controlling nature.

jokes.text: I'm the happiest man in the world. / Oh, great, Bill. Now you can go and fart in front of her. You'll make quite the impression.
jokes.setup: Bill wins the drawing to ask out Maureen
jokes.punchline: Sam's sarcastic reference to Bill's earlier theory about farting and love
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Bill's earlier romantic theory. Sam's sarcasm shows he thinks Bill's dating advice is terrible.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. He wet his paper with spit. What? That's how he wins every time. He just feels for the wet one.
jokes.setup: Bill winning the draw to ask out Maureen
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Bill has been cheating at their random selections
jokes.explanation: Great reversal - innocent Bill revealed as a strategic cheater, recontextualizing previous 'wins'

jokes.text: Wait a minute. He wet his paper with spit. / What? / That's how he wins every time. He just feels for the wet one. / Mrs. Maureen Haverchuck. Has a nice ring to it, don't you think? Ha ha.
jokes.setup: Sam and Neal accuse Bill of cheating in the drawing
jokes.punchline: Bill ignores the accusation and fantasizes about marriage
jokes.explanation: Great character beat showing Bill's sneaky side and immediate romantic fantasy. The contrast between the cheating accusation and his marriage daydream is perfectly Bill.

jokes.text: I heard the words, but your eyes said something else. They said, 'I'm lost. I need your help. Don't listen to me. Guide me.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay tells Mr. Rosso to leave her alone
jokes.punchline: Mr. Rosso's pseudo-psychological interpretation of her rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mr. Rosso - turning clear rejection into a deeper psychological need. His earnest belief in his insight makes it funnier and more cringe.

jokes.text: Tell you what. Why don't I just come and visit you then in the prison-- where you'll be living-- and give you some really good advice, like should you get shanked in the yard or the dining hall? When you have your baby, which prison guard should take care of it? That kind of thing.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso warns Lindsay about her future if she doesn't change
jokes.punchline: His increasingly specific and dark prison scenarios
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of guidance counselor doom predictions. The specificity of shanking locations and prison baby care makes it absurdly dark and funny.

jokes.text: We'll see her in a week, and she'll be all... [SCREECHING]
jokes.setup: Discussion about Maureen becoming one of the popular kids
jokes.punchline: Sam's Body Snatchers alien screech imitation
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical callback to the sci-fi metaphor. Sam's willingness to do the screech shows how seriously they take this threat.

jokes.text: Three's Company is the best show on TV. / Oh? Oh... / Really? / Yes. The Ropers don't want Jack living with 2 girls because he doesn't want any dirty stuff going on in--in his apartment building.
jokes.setup: Eli tries to defend Three's Company to popular girl Vicki
jokes.punchline: His earnest explanation of the show's premise to someone clearly uninterested
jokes.explanation: Classic geek social awkwardness - explaining a sitcom plot to someone who clearly doesn't care. Shows the gap between geek enthusiasm and popular indifference.

jokes.text: Who the hell is 'Jesus H. Garcia'? / It's Hey-Zeus. It's your new identity. / Tell me that picture doesn't look like you. / This picture doesn't look like me, man.
jokes.setup: Daniel receives his fake ID with a Hispanic name
jokes.punchline: Daniel's literal response to Howie's rhetorical challenge
jokes.explanation: Daniel's deadpan literalism in response to Howie's obvious BS. The mispronunciation of Jesus and Daniel's blunt honesty work well together.

jokes.text: Mine looks kinda Chinese. / Yeah. Hey, mine looks Chinese, too. / What's going on with that? / Whoa, whoa, whoa. There's a big Vietnamese community up there in Alberta.
jokes.setup: The fake IDs don't match the users' ethnicities
jokes.punchline: Howie's nonsensical explanation about Vietnamese Canadians
jokes.explanation: Shows Howie's desperation to justify his poor quality fake IDs. His random geographical excuse is appropriately absurd.

jokes.text: It's hard to find matches for you guys. No one in Canada looks like you.
jokes.setup: The fake IDs are obviously wrong for everyone
jokes.punchline: Howie's excuse that Canadians don't look like Americans
jokes.explanation: Howie's absurd excuse for his poor workmanship. The idea that entire countries look different is ridiculous but delivered with conviction.

jokes.text: Yeah. I like it when it's been sitting around for a while, and it gets that skin on the top. Heh.
jokes.setup: Maureen mentions she likes pudding
jokes.punchline: Bill's gross description of pudding skin as a positive feature
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bill - trying to bond over shared interests but making it gross. His enthusiasm for pudding skin reveals his weird food preferences.

jokes.text: Do you want a fake I.D.? So you can go get loaded? / No, Millie, I just wanna hear this band play. / That's why there's records.
jokes.setup: Millie judges Lindsay for wanting a fake ID
jokes.punchline: Millie's practical suggestion that records make live music unnecessary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Millie logic - completely missing the point of live music and social experiences. Her practical solution reveals her social naivety.

jokes.text: I heard the drinking age is something like 10 in Idaho. / Well, I don't think that's right.
jokes.setup: Lindsay makes an obviously false claim about Idaho drinking laws
jokes.punchline: Millie's understated correction
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's desperate attempt to find legal drinking somewhere, met by Millie's reasonable skepticism. The absurdity of age 10 makes it funny.

jokes.text: DON'T PLAY WITH THAT ROOSTER! DON'T PLAY WITH THAT ROOSTER. THAT'S NOT A NICE, PETTY-PET ROOSTER. / I'm sorry. / NO. YOU'RE THE ONE WHO'LL BE SORRY. VERY DANGEROUS.
jokes.setup: Daniel apparently touches or approaches a rooster in Toby's apartment
jokes.punchline: Toby's intense warning about his dangerous pet rooster
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment. The idea of keeping a dangerous rooster as a pet, and Toby's intense concern about it, is perfectly weird and memorable.

jokes.text: I got a green thumb. Don't worry about it.
jokes.setup: Daniel notices what are presumably marijuana plants
jokes.punchline: Toby's casual euphemism about his 'gardening' skills
jokes.explanation: Standard drug growing euphemism. Works for character establishment but not particularly original or memorable.

jokes.text: You can't just go into a store and buy one like it's a toaster. Only corporations have that stuff. It's very expensive.
jokes.setup: Daniel asks about laminating machines for fake IDs
jokes.punchline: Toby's explanation of laminating machine rarity and corporate exclusivity
jokes.explanation: Shows Toby's criminal expertise and the mundane aspects of illegal document creation. The toaster comparison is a nice touch.

jokes.text: There's only one thing that could compete with the Vicki Appleby party. / All-u-can-eat night at the Iron Horse Restaurant. / Of course. So obvious.
jokes.setup: Need something to compete with a popular party
jokes.punchline: The geeks think an all-you-can-eat restaurant is equivalent competition
jokes.explanation: Perfect geek logic - thinking a buffet restaurant can compete with a cool party. Sam's 'so obvious' response shows how seriously they take this strategy.

jokes.text: When we were kids, you were really cute, but, I mean, I never thought you would turn out this hot. / You go to McKinley? / MM-HMM. / It's a pretty good school. I went there. I almost graduated from there a few years ago.
jokes.setup: Toby hits on Lindsay while making her fake ID
jokes.punchline: His admission that he 'almost graduated' from high school
jokes.explanation: Shows Toby's inappropriate behavior and academic failure. The 'almost graduated' detail makes his creepiness even more pathetic.

jokes.text: Hey, you know, actually, she's my girlfriend, man, so do you think maybe you could stop hitting on her? / No, no she's not. / Yeah, I am his girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Daniel claims Lindsay is his girlfriend to stop Toby's advances
jokes.punchline: Lindsay backs up the lie despite their complex relationship status
jokes.explanation: Shows protective Daniel and Lindsay's willingness to play along. Reveals their complicated relationship and her trust in him.

jokes.text: I don't need to ask. I could just get it from my cousin Millie, you know? I was just being polite.
jokes.setup: Lindsay gives Toby her phone number
jokes.punchline: Toby's claim that he was just being polite when he could get her info anyway
jokes.explanation: Shows Toby's creepy persistence and slight threat. His 'politeness' defense doesn't make his behavior less inappropriate.

jokes.text: Did I? I think so. 100 each.
jokes.setup: Toby originally quoted $40 each for fake IDs
jokes.punchline: His price jumping to $100 when challenged
jokes.explanation: Classic scammer behavior - Toby raising prices when he senses desperation

jokes.text: So, was I left back one year. / Well, I know. But still, it wouldn't make you 18. / All right, I was left back twice. Ok, are you happy now? There's no secrets between us.
jokes.setup: Lindsay notices Toby's real ID says he's 18
jokes.punchline: Toby's defensive admission of being held back twice in school
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Toby's academic failures and defensive nature. His 'no secrets between us' comment is inappropriately intimate.

jokes.text: 18, that makes me jail bait. / Hey! You better watch out. I'm gonna call the cops on you. Officer, I didn't know, look at her, she looks like she's 30. / AHH! No I don't!
jokes.setup: Lindsay realizes the age implications
jokes.punchline: Toby's mock defense strategy and Lindsay's outraged reaction to being called 30
jokes.explanation: Shows both Toby's creepiness and Lindsay's vanity. Her outrage at being called 30 is perfectly teenage.

jokes.text: Nobody's home. / You wanna have a little sex? / Sex?
jokes.setup: Harold and Jean realize they're alone
jokes.punchline: Harold's awkward sexual proposition and Jean's surprised response
jokes.explanation: Awkward parental sexuality moment. Harold's directness and Jean's surprise create uncomfortable but relatable humor.

jokes.text: My mom says that it takes a little while for your stomach to tell your brain its full. So you should eat really fast. / Yeah, but the only problem is, the service is really slow. / Yeah, they take forever on purpose, so you'll get full while you wait. / Watch how much bread they bring us.
jokes.setup: The geeks strategize about all-you-can-eat dining
jokes.punchline: Their conspiracy theories about restaurant tactics and bread strategy
jokes.explanation: Perfect geek over-analysis of buffet strategy. Their scientific approach to overeating and paranoia about restaurant tactics is character-perfect.

jokes.text: So, I hear service here is really bad 'cause you don't want us to eat much. / What? I don't own the place. I don't care how much you eat. / Are you sure? / No, actually, I'm a millionaire and I just do this for fun.
jokes.setup: Bill confronts the waitress with his conspiracy theory
jokes.punchline: The waitress's sarcastic response about being a millionaire waitress
jokes.explanation: Great setup/payoff with Bill's paranoia met by perfect sarcasm. The waitress's comeback is sharp and realistic for someone dealing with weird customers.

jokes.text: Where's your mustache, Jesus? / It's Hay-Soos, and I shaved it. / Too bad. You looked better with it. / Gracias. / Señor.
jokes.setup: Bouncer questions Daniel about his fake ID photo
jokes.punchline: Daniel's quick improvisation about shaving and their Spanish exchange
jokes.explanation: Shows Daniel's quick thinking under pressure. The Spanish exchange adds authenticity to his fake identity performance.

jokes.text: Do you want to see my I.D.? / No, you're good.
jokes.setup: Lindsay offers to show her obviously fake ID
jokes.punchline: Bouncer waves her through without checking
jokes.explanation: Ironic moment showing arbitrary enforcement of ID checking. Lindsay's eagerness to show her fake ID contrasts with not needing it.

jokes.text: Those I.D.s look nuts! Business must be pretty bad if we're supposed to let them in.
jokes.setup: The obviously fake IDs get people into the bar
jokes.punchline: Bouncer's admission that business is so bad they'll accept anything
jokes.explanation: Great behind-the-scenes look at bar economics. The bouncer's honesty about lowered standards for business reasons is both funny and realistic.

jokes.text: All the way from McKinley High School, we've got the key members of another smokin' band we like to call 'Creation.' Daniel Desario, Ken Miller, Nick Andopolis, Kim Kelly, and their manager, Miss Lindsay Weir.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso spotting his students at the bar
jokes.punchline: His mortifying public introduction outing them as underage
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe climax - Mr. Rosso's well-meaning enthusiasm destroying their cover

jokes.text: I can't let you serve our guests plain old beer. Why don't you bring them some of your finest pop, on the house. / OH, I GOT THOSE UNDER-AGE DRINKING BLUES.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso spots his students at the bar and intervenes
jokes.punchline: His request for soda instead of beer, followed by an impromptu blues song about underage drinking
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Mr. Rosso moment combining his authority with his musical pretensions. The improvised blues song about the situation is perfectly cringe and memorable.

jokes.text: Hey, guys. Um, hey, do you mind if I go over and sit with those guys today? / No, no, not at all. / Do you want to come and sit with me? Over there? / Yeah, I mean, you don't have to sit here forever if you don't want to. / Yeah, but we like it here. / Yeah, go have fun with your friends. We'll be here if you need us.
jokes.setup: Maureen asks to sit with the popular kids
jokes.punchline: The geeks' gracious but sad acceptance of being abandoned
jokes.explanation: Heartbreaking character moment showing the geeks' maturity and kindness despite being hurt. Their generous response to rejection is both funny and touching.

jokes.text: Just remember, the jocks are only after one thing. And be careful around Vicki. She's a wolf in sheep's clothing. And remember, don't believe everything they say about people in the school. 'Cause they don't always take the time to get to know everyone. / Guys, I'm just sitting 3 tables over, I'm not moving back to Florida.
jokes.setup: Neal gives Maureen extensive warnings about the popular kids
jokes.punchline: Maureen's practical observation about the short physical distance
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between Neal's dramatic warnings and Maureen's practical perspective. Shows how the geeks catastrophize social changes.

jokes.text: Did you guys happen to see Three's Company last night? [LAUGHS] It was so funny. / What happened? / I don't really remember, there was a lot going on. / Oh. / Joyce DeWitt is really hot. If I was Jack, I'd have a very hard time choosing between the 2 lovely ladies on the show because, you know, one's a brunette, one's a blonde,
jokes.setup: Harris tries to start a conversation about Three's Company
jokes.punchline: His admission he doesn't remember what happened, followed by Bill's overly analytical attraction assessment
jokes.explanation: Shows the geeks' social awkwardness in trying to make conversation. Harris's failed attempt and Bill's inappropriate analysis create mild cringe comedy.

jokes.text: If I was Jack, I'd have a very hard time choosing between the 2 lovely ladies on the show because, you know, one's a brunette, one's a blonde,
jokes.setup: Bill trying to make conversation about Three's Company
jokes.punchline: His detailed analysis of his attraction to the female characters
jokes.explanation: Classic Bill oversharing - turning TV discussion into uncomfortable personal revelation

tmdb_overview: Lindsay reluctantly agrees to come over to Nick's house while his parents are out of town. Sam hopes to become lab partners with Cindy, but becomes jealous when Bill is named Cindy's lab partner.
